Integration.description:
Integrations connect Gorgias with external services (e.g. Gmail, Shopify, chat systems).

There are official Gorgias integrations that can be found in the Integrations page in the helpdesk and there are custom HTTP integrations.

Most integrations use HTTP APIs.

business_hours_id.description: The ID of the business hour the phone integration is associated with. If null, the integration will use the default business hours.

http.description: Only available for HTTP integrations, defines the configuration of the integration.

form.description: Data to send to the external service. It can either be a raw JSON object (if `request_content_type` is `application/json`) or a key-value object representing form data (if `request_content_type` is `application/x-www-form-urlencoded`). You can use variables like {{ticket.customer.email}} as values for the request. Check all properties from a [customer](#the-customer-object), or review the list of [Gorgias variables](https://docs.gorgias.com/macros/macro-variables#gorgias_variables) for inspiration.

method.description: HTTP method expected by the external service.

request_content_type.description: Content type of the outgoing request to the external service.

response_content_type.description: Content type of the incoming response from the external service.

triggers.description: Events that will trigger the request to the external service.

ticket-assignment-updated.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when the ticket assignee is changed.

ticket-created.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when a ticket is created.

ticket-handed-over.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when a ticket is handed over to another user. Further configuration is required.

ticket-message-created.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when a new message is created in a ticket.

ticket-message-failed.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when the message fails to send.

ticket-self-unsnoozed.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when the snooze delay ends.

ticket-status-updated.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when the ticket status is updated.

ticket-updated.description: Whether to trigger the HTTP integration when a ticket is updated.

url.description: URL of the external service. You can use variables like {{ticket.customer.email}} to pass customer data to the integration. Check all properties from a [customer](#the-customer-object), or review the list of [Gorgias variables](https://docs.gorgias.com/macros/macro-variables#gorgias_variables) for inspiration.

managed.description: Whether integration is managed by Gorgias.

name.description: Name of the integration. Usually the email address, Facebook page name, etc.

type.description: Type of integration.

updated_datetime.description: When the integration was last updated.

Job.description:
A job is an object used to perform long-running tasks. For example, closing 10k tickets, exporting 500k tickets, and more. 

Jobs are processed asynchronously, meaning that once a job is created, it will run in background on Gorgias's servers. According to the type and size of the task, a job can take a few minutes or several hours to be executed. You can use our API to check the status of the job.

Jobs can be canceled at any time but be aware that if a job already started, changes done by this one won't be reverted. For example, if you created a job to close 10k tickets and you cancel it at some point, the already closed tickets won't be reopened.

cancel_requested_datetime.description: When the job cancellation was requested.

info.description: Data concerning the progress of the job. E.g: how many objects the job has already processed.

locked_datetime.description: When the job was locked. A job is only locked while it's actively running.

meta.description: Metadata associated with the job. You can use this parameter to store structured information (key-value data) about the job. This field is not used by Gorgias.

example.description: This job closes all tickets created in 2018

apply_and_close.description: Whether the ticket should be closed once the macro is applied on it. Only applies to `applyMacro` jobs.

context.description: Object (key-value) containing extra context for the job

end_datetime.description: End of the interval from which to select tickets when the parameter `view` is used.

macro_id.description: The ID of the macro to apply on the selected tickets. Only applies to `applyMacro` jobs.

start_datetime.description: Beginning of the interval from which to select tickets when the parameter `view` is used.

ticket_ids.description: A list of ticket IDs to be processed by the job.

updates.description: Object (key-value) containing all the changes to apply on the selected tickets. Only applies to `updateTicket` jobs.

url.description: The path to the file to import.

view.description: A [view](#the-view-object)-like object used to select the tickets to be processed by the job.

view_id.description: The ID of an existing [view](#the-view-object) used to select the tickets to be processed by the job.

scheduled_datetime.description: When the job was scheduled to be started. A job cannot be scheduled more than 60 minutes in the future. If unspecified, the job is queued for execution immediately after creation.

status.description: The status of the job. Options: cancel_requested, canceled, done, errored, fatal_errored, pending, running, scheduled

type.description: The type of the job. Options: applyMacro, deleteTicket, exportTicket, importMacro, exportMacro, updateTicket, exportTicketDrilldown, exportConvertCampaignSalesDrilldown

user_id.description: The ID of the user who created this job.

Macro.description:
A macro (canned response) is a list of [actions](#the-macroaction-object) that can be applied to tickets to modify them, and/or reply to them.Macros can be applied manually by agents or automatically by rules.
For example, a single macro can add a tag to the ticket, assign an agent to it and send a pre-defined response.

Rule.description:
Rules allow you to automatically perform actions on tickets when specific events occur. Rules are executed in the order of their `priority` field, with the rules having the higher priority being executed first.

For example, you can use rules to automatically tag your tickets, assign agents, apply macros, send messages and more.

code_ast.description: The logic of the rule (as an ESTree [AST](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abstract_syntax_tree) representation). Generated from the code if not specified.

description.example: Automatically close all tickets from Amazon because there is nothing to do for them. We still want to keep them to be able to search.

event_types.description: A list of comma separated events that this rule will be executed on.

priority.description: Order of execution of the rule. Rules with higher priorities are executed first.

updated_datetime.description: When the rule was last updated.

A tag is used to group tickets sharing one or several characteristics to easily retrieve them.Tags are used in [views](#the-view-object), [rules](#the-rule-object) and in [statistics](#the-statistic-object) to filter out specific tickets.

A ticket is a conversation between your support agents and one of your customers.

TicketMessage.description:
A ticket-message represents a message (an email, an SMS, a Facebook comment, chat message, etc..) under a normalized format.
There are 3 types of messages:
  - Outgoing messages: messages sent by your company (by rules or support agents) to your customers.
  - Incoming messages: messages sent by your [customers](#the-customer-object) to your company.
  - Internal notes: internal messages sent between support agents ([users](#the-user-object)). Internal notes are not sent/shared with your customers.

A ticket-message is associated with only one ticket.

actions.description: A list of actions executed before the message was sent. These actions are set by macros.
actions.title: Actions

attachments.description: A list of files attached to the message.
attachments.title: Attachments

auth_customer_identity.description: Identity of the authenticated customer, if any, in the context of a message sent on a channel that does not require authentication such as email, SMS or Gorgias chat.This attribute is used to differentiate messages sent by unauthenticated and authenticated customers.

body_html.description: The full HTML version of the body of the message, if any.
body_html.title: Body Html
body_html.example:
Hello,

I can't place an order on your site, it says: I don't have enough credit.
How can I add some credits?

Cheers,
John Doe

body_text.description: The full text version of the body of the message, if any.
body_text.title: Body Text
body_text.example:
Hello,

        I can't place an order on your site, it says: I don't have enough credit.
        How can I add some credits?

        Cheers,
        John Doe

integration_id.title: Integration Id

is_retriable.description: It determines if the message can be retried or not.
is_retriable.title: Is Retriable

last_sending_error.description: Details of the last error encountered when Gorgias attempted to send the message.

message_id.title: Message Id

meta.description:
Metadata associated with the message.

You can use this field to store structured information (key-value data) about the message.

Reserved keys:

- `current_page`: The page from where the user is currently chatting.
- `relevant_content_indexes`: Indexes containing relevant text of the message.
- `is_quick_reply`: Whether the message is a "quick reply".
- `campaigns`: ID of the chat campaign the message is replying to.
- `campaigns_id`: ID of the chat campaign that sent this message.
- `self_service_flow`: An object containing metadata of the self-service flow associated to the message.

View.description:
A view allows you to filter and sort the tickets of your account according to one or more criteria. For example, you can have a view that displays tickets having the tag `urgent`, or a view that display closed tickets, etc...
 Views are not like inboxes because a ticket can be present in 1 or more views if this one matches criterias of these views.

fields.description: List of object's attributes to be displayed in the UI of our applications.

filters.description: The logic used to filter the items to be displayed in the view (as JavaScript code).

order_by.description: Name of the object's attribute used to sort the items of the view.

order_dir.description: Sort direction of the items displayed in the view. Options: `asc` or `desc`.

search.description: Text used to search for items matching the query.

items.description: A `Team` represents a group of [users](#user-object). Teams are used with the "auto-assign tickets" feature: you can assign a ticket to a team (manually of via rules), then it will be automatically assigned to someone who is available in the given team.

decoration.description: Object describing how the team appears on the webpage. Currently only supports `emoji` field that shows before the team's name.

items.description: Users represent people working for your company. They can be support agents, support specialists, support managers, directors, etc... Users are distinguished by their role.

sso.description: Name of the Single Sign-On provider the user can use to log in

slug.description: DEPRECATED - URL-compatible name of the view.

type.description: Type of objects the view is applied on.

visibility.description:
Visibility of the view.

Possible values are:

- `public`: any user of your helpdesk can see it.
- `shared`: users listed under `shared_with_users` attribute and teams listed under `shared_with_teams` attribute can see it. All admins and lead agents will see it as well, even if they are not explicitly listed in the `shared_with_users` attribute.
- `private`: only the single user listed under `shared_with_users` attribute can see it.

In case both customers have data for the same integration a conflict will occur with the API returning an error.

parameters.description: The ID of the customer to merge (which will be merged into the targeted customer).
parameters.name: source_id

parameters.description: The ID of the targeted customer (which will still be here after the merge).
parameters.name: target_id

post.description: Create a job and schedule its execution. Jobs are processed asynchronously, meaning that once a job is created, it will run in background on Gorgias's servers. According to the type and size of the task, a job can take a few minutes or several hours to be executed. You can use our API to check the status of the job.

post.description:
Create a message and either:
  - not send it if the attribute `sent_datetime` is filled.
  If you import a message that was already sent, make sure to set this value.
  - send it to the recipients if the attribute `sent_datetime` is empty.
  - add it to the ticket as an internal note if you specified this channel.
  Internal notes are not sent to your customers.

When a message is created, it does not mean that this one has been sent because the creation and sending process are decoupled. Messages are basically sent asynchronously. Once a message has been sent, Gorgias will automatically update the message to fill the time when it was sent (`sent_datetime`).

parameters.description: The ID of the ticket to create a message in.
parameters.name: ticket_id

parameters.description:
Policy applied on external actions associated with the message if they failed.
Three actions are possible:
  - force: bypass the failed action and try to execute the remaining one and to send the message
  - retry: retry the failed action
  - cancel: delete the TicketMessage
parameters.name: action
